data.title: J Jill (JILL) Q4 Loss And Margin Compression Test Bullish Valuation Narrative
data.description:
J.Jill (JILL) closed out FY 2026 with Q4 revenue of US$138.4 million and a basic EPS loss of US$0.23, while trailing twelve month figures show revenue of US$596.5 million and EPS of US$1.84. Over recent quarters, revenue has ranged between US$150.5 million and US$153.9 million with quarterly EPS between US$0.61 and US$0.76, and trailing twelve month EPS has moved between US$1.84 and US$2.86 as net income shifted from US$39.5 million to US$27.9 million. For investors, the mix of full year profitability alongside a softer final quarter puts the focus squarely on how margins are holding up into the new fiscal year.

See our full analysis for J.Jill.

With the latest numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up with the widely held bullish and cautious narratives around J.Jill and where those stories might need a rethink.

See what the community is saying about J.Jill

Advertisement

Full year profit, but Q4 slips into a US$3.5 million loss

  • Across FY 2026, trailing twelve month net income sits at US$27.9 million on US$596.5 million of revenue. Q4 on its own swung to a US$3.5 million loss on US$138.4 million of revenue, after three earlier quarters each produced US$9.2 million to US$11.7 million of profit.
  • Consensus narrative highlights efforts to tighten operations and inventory, and the numbers partly support that. However, the shift from a trailing net margin of 6.5% a year ago to 4.7% now shows that cost pressure and promotions are still biting.
    • Quarterly EPS moved from US$0.76 in Q1 to a loss of US$0.23 in Q4, while trailing twelve month EPS stepped down from US$2.64 in Q4 FY 2025 to US$1.84 in Q4 FY 2026. This lines up with concerns about margin compression even as the business remains profitable over the year.
    • Same store sales data in FY 2026, with declines of 5.7% in Q1 and around 1% in Q2 and Q3, sits awkwardly against the consensus view that assortment tweaks and omnichannel investments will steadily lift demand. Investors may want to watch whether those initiatives eventually show up in these store level metrics.

Modest revenue base, falling margin versus prior year

  • On a trailing basis, revenue was US$596.5 million in Q4 FY 2026 compared with US$610.9 million reported a year earlier, while trailing net income moved from US$39.5 million to US$27.9 million. This is consistent with the margin shift from 6.5% to 4.7% flagged in the risk summary.
  • Bears argue that slow top line progress and heavier promotions could lock the business into low growth, and the figures give some support to that concern. They also show the company still producing profits over the full year.
    • Over the last four reported quarters, quarterly revenue hovered in a narrow band between US$138.4 million and US$154.0 million, while same store sales growth stayed negative across Q1 to Q3 FY 2026. This echoes the bearish view that demand from the core middle aged customer may not be expanding quickly.
    • The trailing margin dip from 6.5% to 4.7% aligns with commentary about higher promotions and cost inflation, yet the fact that the margin remains positive runs counter to the harshest bearish worry that profitability could vanish quickly if store traffic softens further.
Bears focusing on an aging customer base and weaker like for like sales may want to see how that thesis lines up with the detailed scenario work in the 🐻 J.Jill Bear Case.

Low 6.2x P/E and DCF fair value of US$28.64

  • With the share price at US$11.46, the trailing P/E of 6.2x sits well under the cited US Specialty Retail average of 19.3x and peer average of 31.7x. The stock is described as trading about 60% below an indicated DCF fair value of US$28.64.
  • Bullish investors point to this gap and to forecasts of roughly 17.5% yearly earnings growth as evidence that the market is underestimating the business. The same dataset also notes that revenue is only expected to grow about 1% per year, so the enthusiasm rests heavily on margin improvement rather than a rapid expansion in sales.
    • The difference between the US$11.46 share price and the DCF fair value of US$28.64 is wide, and when set next to a low P/E it fits the bullish claim that the stock screens as inexpensive. It also means those investors are relying on the company lifting profitability from the current 4.7% net margin closer to the higher levels described in their scenarios.
    • Analysts have a reference price target of US$16.33, above the current market price yet well below the DCF fair value figure. This underlines that even supportive forecasts around earnings do not all assume the share price quickly closes the entire valuation gap.
Anyone weighing those valuation numbers against the recent margin pressure can get a clearer sense of the optimistic case in the latest bull thesis for J.Jill via the 🐂 J.Jill Bull Case.

data.description:

Recent coverage has focused on broader questions around sustained growth, competitive pressures across Latin American e commerce and digital payments, and how these themes are being reflected in market pricing.

Approach 1: MercadoLibre Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A DCF model estimates what a company could be worth by projecting its future cash flows, then discounting those projections back to today using a required rate of return. It focuses on the cash the business may generate for shareholders rather than just headline earnings.

For MercadoLibre, Simply Wall St uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model in US$. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $10.96b. Analyst estimates and extrapolated figures feed into a ten year path, with projected free cash flow in 2035 of around $11.60b, based on the supplied forecasts and extensions beyond the analyst horizon.

Bringing all those future cash flows back to today produces an estimated intrinsic value of about $2,277 per share. Against the recent share price of roughly $1,729, the model suggests the stock trades at a 24.1% discount to that DCF estimate, which indicates a material valuation gap on this approach.

Result: UNDERVALUED

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a helpful way to see what you are paying for each dollar of earnings. A higher P/E typically reflects stronger growth expectations or lower perceived risk, while a lower P/E can point to more modest growth assumptions or higher risk.

data.title: Insider Buyers Lose UK£32k As Headlam Group Sheds UK£5.9m
data.description:
The recent 16% drop in Headlam Group plc's (LON:HEAD) stock could come as a blow to insiders who purchased UK£58.2k worth of stock at an average buy price of UK£0.83 over the past 12 months. Insiders buy with the expectation to see their investments rise in value over a period of time. However, recent losses have rendered their above investment worth UK£26.4k which is not ideal.

While we would never suggest that investors should base their decisions solely on what the directors of a company have been doing, we would consider it foolish to ignore insider transactions altogether.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Advertisement

Headlam Group Insider Transactions Over The Last Year

The Interim Executive Chairman Stephen Bird made the biggest insider purchase in the last 12 months. That single transaction was for UK£41k worth of shares at a price of UK£0.82 each. That means that an insider was happy to buy shares at above the current price of UK£0.38. It's very possible they regret the purchase, but it's more likely they are bullish about the company. To us, it's very important to consider the price insiders pay for shares. As a general rule, we feel more positive about a stock if insiders have bought shares at above current prices, because that suggests they viewed the stock as good value, even at a higher price.

Headlam Group insiders may have bought shares in the last year, but they didn't sell any. The chart below shows insider transactions (by companies and individuals) over the last year. By clicking on the graph below, you can see the precise details of each insider transaction!

Check out our latest analysis for Headlam Group

Headlam Group is not the only stock insiders are buying. So take a peek at this free list of under-the-radar companies with insider buying.

Does Headlam Group Boast High Insider Ownership?

Looking at the total insider shareholdings in a company can help to inform your view of whether they are well aligned with common shareholders. We usually like to see fairly high levels of insider ownership. From looking at our data, insiders own UK£474k worth of Headlam Group stock, about 1.6% of the company. We do note, however, it is possible insiders have an indirect interest through a private company or other corporate structure. We consider this fairly low insider ownership.

So What Do The Headlam Group Insider Transactions Indicate?

There haven't been any insider transactions in the last three months -- that doesn't mean much. But insiders have shown more of an appetite for the stock, over the last year. We'd like to see bigger individual holdings. However, we don't see anything to make us think Headlam Group insiders are doubting the company. In addition to knowing about insider transactions going on, it's beneficial to identify the risks facing Headlam Group. In terms of investment risks, we've identified 1 warning sign with Headlam Group and understanding this should be part of your investment process.

If you would prefer to check out another company -- one with potentially superior financials -- then do not miss this free list of interesting companies, that have HIGH return on equity and low debt.

For the purposes of this article, insiders are those individuals who report their transactions to the relevant regulatory body. We currently account for open market transactions and private dispositions of direct interests only, but not derivative transactions or indirect interests.

data.title: GPI (BIT:GPI) Margin Squeeze In 1H 2025 Tests Bullish Earnings Growth Narrative
data.description:
GPI (BIT:GPI) has released its FY 2025 numbers with first half revenue of €257.9 million and basic EPS of €0.13, setting the tone for how the year is shaping up after a trailing twelve month EPS of €0.55 on revenue of €546.2 million. Over recent periods the company has seen revenue move from €236.4 million in 1H 2024 to €273.5 million in 2H 2024 and then to €257.9 million in 1H 2025, while basic EPS has shifted from €0.11 to €0.42 and then €0.13. This leaves investors focused on how much of the earnings story is coming through cleanly in underlying margins.

See our full analysis for GPI.

With the latest earnings now on the table, the next step is to weigh these figures against the prevailing market narratives to see which views hold up and which get pushed back.

See what the community is saying about GPI

Advertisement

3% net margin puts profitability in focus

  • Over the last twelve months GPI earned €16.2 million of net income on €546.2 million of revenue, which works out to a 3% net profit margin that is flagged as lower than the prior year and relatively thin for a software led healthcare group.
  • Analysts' consensus view sees this current 3% margin as a starting point for improvement, but the data highlights a tension between that bullish margin story and recent results:
    • Net income from ongoing operations has been fairly modest in recent half years, at €3.1 million in 1H 2024, €12.1 million in 2H 2024 and €3.7 million in 1H 2025, compared with much larger contributions from discontinued operations earlier in 2024.
    • The consensus narrative talks about higher recurring software and international revenues supporting a structurally higher EBITDA margin. However, the current 3% net margin shows that any margin expansion still needs to come through clearly in reported earnings.
🐻 GPI Bear Case

Forecast 30.6% earnings growth vs €16.46 share price

  • Consensus forecasts point to earnings growth of about 30.6% per year over the next three years, compared with historical earnings growth of 6.3% per year, while the current share price of €16.46 sits above a DCF fair value estimate of €8.34 and below an analyst price target of €18.57.
  • What stands out in the bullish narrative is the gap between strong earnings growth expectations and current valuation signals:
    • Support for the bullish view comes from the earnings track record, with earnings growing 6.3% per year over five years and 6.4% over the last year, and from forecasts that assume profit margins rise from 3.0% to 6.1% and earnings reach €38.6 million by about 2028.
    • In contrast, the reported P/E of 29.4x and a DCF fair value of €8.34 suggest the market is already assigning a premium relative to modelled cash flows and to the analyst target of €18.57. This means the delivery of that 30.6% earnings growth is central to justifying today’s price.
🐂 GPI Bull Case

P/E of 29.4x with weaker coverage ratios

  • The stock trades on a trailing P/E of 29.4x, ahead of the Global Healthcare Services industry average of 27.1x and a peer average of 24x, while at the same time interest payments and a 3.04% dividend are flagged as not well covered by current earnings and free cash flow.
  • Critics highlight that this richer multiple sits alongside balance sheet and cash flow pressures rather than against a clean financial base:
    • The risk summary points to interest coverage as a concern, meaning current earnings leave limited room after servicing debt, which matters given the mention of a sizeable net financial position around €340 million.
    • The dividend coverage flag, together with thin 3% net margins, suggests that cash needed for CapEx and expansion has to compete with shareholder payouts. This may make the current premium P/E more sensitive to any slowdown in earnings or free cash flow.

Next Steps

A Discounted Cash Flow model projects the cash a company may generate in the future and then discounts those projected cash flows back to today to estimate what the business might be worth right now.

The DCF model used here is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, which starts with analyst estimates and then extends them further out. For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful yardstick because it links what you pay per share to the earnings that each share currently generates. Investors usually accept a higher P/E when they expect stronger growth or see lower risk, and a lower P/E when growth expectations are more modest or risks are higher.

data.title: The Tactical Trio: One Pure Quantum Blueprint, One Annealing Progression, One Cash Engine Composition
data.description:
Quantum computing is moving from the lab into real-world use at the same time as investors are wrestling with higher energy costs, sticky inflation signals, and changing interest-rate expectations across major economies. While bond markets, central banks, and commodity prices keep shifting, this screener focuses on companies at the center of quantum hardware, software, and research. It offers exposure to a very specific long term technology trend rather than broad macro swings. In this article, you will see 3 of the most interesting stocks from the Quantum Computing Stocks screener and what sets each one apart.

Advertisement

IonQ (IONQ)

Overview: IonQ operates quantum computing systems that clients can access through major cloud platforms and its own services, while also working on quantum safe communications, detection systems, and specialized hardware, support, and consulting contracts. The company partners with institutions such as the University of Chicago and runs its headquarters out of College Park, Maryland.

Operations: IonQ currently generates about US$130 million in revenue from Computer Services, with around US$87 million from the United States, US$17 million from Switzerland, and US$26 million from other international markets.

Market Cap: US$9.7b

IonQ is one of the purest ways to get exposure to quantum computing, with all of its revenue tied to quantum related services and a customer base that includes major cloud platforms and research institutions. Recent contracts in Europe and Asia and US federal work on secure communications, together with guidance pointing to higher revenue in 2026, indicate that demand for its technology is spreading across governments and enterprises. At the same time, IonQ is still unprofitable, relies heavily on external funding, has seen shareholder dilution, and operates with a relatively new leadership team, so the story depends heavily on execution. The key issue for investors is how this mix of rapid commercial progress and high risk could affect the next phase of quantum adoption.

IonQ’s rapid commercial traction and pure quantum focus can look exciting, but the real story sits in how that growth case lines up with expectations in the analyst forecasts for IonQ , and one potential blind spot investors often miss

D-Wave Quantum (QBTS)

Overview: D-Wave Quantum builds and operates quantum computers that tackle complex optimization problems for commercial and government clients, offering access through its Leap cloud service, on premises Advantage systems, and software tools that help developers build and run quantum applications.

Operations: D-Wave Quantum generates about US$24.6 million in revenue from Internet Software & Services, supported by customers across markets including Germany, the United States, Japan, Canada, Switzerland, and other regions.

Market Cap: US$4.8b

D-Wave Quantum stands out for turning quantum annealing into real commercial products. More than 100 revenue generating customers use its QCaaS platform, hybrid solvers, and high value system sales to tackle scheduling, logistics, and portfolio optimization problems. At the same time, the company remains unprofitable, carries externally funded liabilities, and depends on relatively few large hardware deals alongside a young board. This raises questions about how quickly usage will ramp to cover rising R&D and operating costs. Analysts still expect revenue growth above 40% a year and see potential in its dual track annealing and gate model roadmap. The key tension is how those expectations line up with execution risk, cash usage, and the market’s view on quantum annealing versus rival approaches.

D-Wave’s effort to turn quantum annealing into real revenue is gaining attention. The more important story, however, lies in how its cash needs, customer concentration, and roadmap fit together in the 1 key reward and 4 important warning signs

Meta Platforms (META)

Overview: Meta Platforms runs some of the world’s largest social and messaging apps, including Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, Messenger, Threads and Meta AI. It is also expanding into virtual and augmented reality through its Reality Labs segment, which sells Meta Quest headsets and AI enabled wearables like Ray Ban Meta glasses.

Operations: Meta generates about US$201b in revenue, with around US$199b from its Family of Apps and about US$2.2b from Reality Labs.

Market Cap: US$1,356.8b

For a quantum themed screener, Meta is interesting because it combines a large cash generating social media and messaging business with investment in AI infrastructure, custom chips and AR or VR hardware that could benefit from future advances in compute. Analysts see a gap between the current share price and their assessments of value, while the company reports high profit margins and strong returns on equity alongside significant spending on data centers and AI models. At the same time, regulators are testing new legal theories around social media “addiction,” privacy and competition, and Meta is exposed to advertising cycles. How that mix of legal risk, capital intensive AI initiatives and the economics of the existing business interacts is a key consideration in evaluating the investment case.

Meta’s cash rich social platforms and substantial AI or hardware investments can be hard to piece together. See how that mix is shaping up in the analysis report for Meta Platforms

The latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of CA$351.7 million. These later years are extrapolated by Simply Wall St rather than based on additional analyst estimates, which is important to keep in mind given the uncertainties for a mining name at this stage.

Pulling all those projected cash flows together and discounting them back to today gives an estimated intrinsic value of CA$220.52 per share. Against a current share price of about CA$41.43, the model indicates the stock is 81.2% undervalued according to this DCF framework.

For profitable, established firms, investors usually look for a P/B ratio that reflects both how fast the business might grow and how predictable that growth is, with higher growth and lower perceived risk often justifying a higher multiple.
